The '''64th British Academy Film Awards''', more commonly known as the [[BAFTAs]], were held on 13 February 2011 at the [[Royal Opera House]] in [[London]], honouring the best national and foreign films of [[2010 in film|2010]].<ref name="Winners">{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-12445061 |title=Bafta Film Awards 2011: Winners |publisher=[[BBC News]] |date=13 February 2011 |access-date=13 February 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite press release |url=https://www.bafta.org/media-centre/press-releases/film-awards-2011-winners-list |title=Film Awards 2011: Winners List |publisher=[[British Academy of Film and Television Arts]] |date=13 February 2011 |access-date=13 February 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite press release |url=https://www.bafta.org/media-centre/press-releases/film-awards-2011-winners-announced |title=Film Awards 2011: Winners Announced |publisher=[[British Academy of Film and Television Arts]] |date=13 February 2011 |access-date=13 February 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-12432378 |title=King's Speech reigns over Bafta awards |publisher=[[BBC News]] |date=13 February 2011 |access-date=13 February 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2011/feb/13/baftas-2011-the-kings-speech |title=Baftas 2011: The King's Speech sweeps the board |last=Brown |first=Mark |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=13 February 2011 |access-date=13 February 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/arts-entertainment/films/news/king-s-speech-rules-the-baftas-with-historic-seven-awards-2213857.html |title=King's Speech rules the Baftas with historic seven awards |last=Sharp |first=Rob |work=[[The Independent]] |date=13 February 2011 |access-date=13 February 2011}}</ref> Presented by the [[British Academy of Film and Television Arts]], accolades are handed out for the best feature-length film and documentaries of any nationality that were screened at British cinemas in 2010.

The nominations were announced on 18 January 2011.<ref>{{cite web |title=British Academy Film Awards in 2011 – Longlist |url=http://static.bafta.org/files/longlist-1011-774.pdf |website=bafta.org |publisher=British Academy of Film and Television Arts |access-date=25 January 2026 |ref=Longlist |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110124155857/http://static.bafta.org:80/files/longlist-1011-774.pdf |archive-date=24 January 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-12213618 |title=The King's Speech leads Bafta field |publisher=[[BBC News]] |date=18 January 2011 |access-date=18 January 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2011/jan/18/bafta-nominations-kings-speech-2011 |title=Baftas 2011: The King's Speech leads parade with 14 nominations |last=Brooks |first=Xan |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=18 January 2011 |access-date=18 January 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/arts-entertainment/films/the-king-s-speech-leads-british-academy-award-nominees-2188371.html |title='The King's Speech' leads British Academy Award nominees |work=[[The Independent]] |date=18 January 2011 |access-date=18 January 2011}}</ref> ''[[The King's Speech]]'' earned the most nominations with fourteen and won seven, including Best Film, Outstanding British Film, Best Actor for [[Colin Firth]], Best Supporting Actor for [[Geoffrey Rush]], Best Supporting Actress for [[Helena Bonham Carter]], and Best Original Screenplay for [[David Seidler]].<ref name="Winners"/> [[Natalie Portman]] won Best Actress for ''[[Black Swan (film)|Black Swan]]'' and [[David Fincher]] won Best Director for ''[[The Social Network]]''.<ref name="Winners"/>

* '''Sir [[Christopher Lee]]'''<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-12395181 |title=Christopher Lee to receive Bafta Fellowship |publisher=[[BBC News]] |date=8 February 2011 |access-date=8 February 2011}}</ref>

{{Main|BAFTA Outstanding British Contribution to Cinema Award}}
